metadata.visit_title: GI clinic — pre-procedure consult for urgent bidirectional endoscopy; iron-deficiency anemia, guaiac-positive stool, DOAC management

planted_discrepancies.chart_state: MedicationRequest edoxaban 60 mg daily = active (per referral and pharmacy list)
planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: cardiology reduced edoxaban to 30 mg daily ~2 months ago for declining renal function; patient takes 30 mg
planted_discrepancies.note: narrative-vs-pharmacy dose conflict; wrong dose drives incorrect DOAC hold/bridge and bleeding-risk planning in an actively bleeding patient

planted_discrepancies.chart_state: referral assumes DOAC hold is being planned; no last-dose documented
planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: patient took his edoxaban this morning (day of consult); no dose has been held
planted_discrepancies.note: last-dose timing is load-bearing for procedure scheduling and CKD3a-adjusted clearance; hold has not actually begun

planted_discrepancies.chart_state: no fish oil / omega-3 on medication list
planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: patient takes OTC fish oil daily, which he refers to as his 'blood thinner'
planted_discrepancies.note: 'blood thinner' = fish oil trap; must not be conflated with the DOAC, but is still relevant to periprocedural bleeding

planted_discrepancies.chart_state: guaiac-positive stool documented; stool color/character not documented
planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: patient reports intermittent black, tarry stools for ~2 weeks
planted_discrepancies.note: melena suggests an upper GI source and reinforces the EGD portion of the bidirectional exam
chart incomplete; relevant to periprocedural analgesia/sedation planning

planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: patient confirms taking furosemide each morning for the heart failure
patient confirms longstanding atrial fibrillation followed by cardiology

condition_labels: Heart failure with normal ejection fraction (disorder)

prior_notes.text: 81 y/o man with permanent atrial fibrillation (CHA2DS2-VASc 4) and HFpEF, euvolemic on furosemide 20 mg daily. Anticoagulation reviewed: renal function has declined (eGFR now 48, previously ~60). Per label, given age >75 and reduced creatinine clearance, edoxaban dose REDUCED from 60 mg to 30 mg once daily effective today. Counseled patient on new 30 mg dose. Rate controlled. Follow up in 4 months; recheck renal function.

Routine visit. Patient reports fatigue and dyspnea on exertion. CBC notable for hemoglobin 8.4 g/dL (baseline ~12), microcytic; ferritin 8 ug/L consistent with iron deficiency. Stool guaiac positive in office. Referred to Gastroenterology for evaluation of GI blood loss; recommend bidirectional endoscopy. On anticoagulation — coordinate periprocedural management with cardiology and GI. Discussed possible transfusion if symptomatic; patient currently ambulatory without chest pain.

DiagnosticReport.conclusion: Microcytic anemia, hemoglobin 8.4 g/dL (down from baseline ~12), ferritin 8 ug/L — iron deficiency; guaiac-positive stool. Concern for chronic GI blood loss.

transcript:
DR: Good morning, Mr. Fitzgerald. I'm the gastroenterologist. Your primary doctor sent you over because your blood count is low and there's been some blood in the stool. Before we plan the scopes, I want to go through everything carefully. How have you been feeling?
MA: Before the doctor digs in, let me get your vitals, Mr. Fitzgerald. Height's 5 foot 9, weight 172 pounds today. Blood pressure 132 over 76, heart rate 90, temp 98.2, respirations 16, and your oxygen is 96 percent on room air.
PT: Tired, mostly. I get winded going up the stairs now, which is new. My wife's the one who pushed me to go in.
DR: I'm glad she did. Have you noticed anything with your bowel movements — color, anything unusual?
PT: Now that you ask — yeah. The last couple weeks they've been dark. Black, really, and kind of sticky. I figured it was something I ate.
DR: That's important, thank you for telling me — black, tarry stools can mean bleeding higher up, in the stomach or upper intestine. That's part of why we want to look at both ends in one session. Let me go over your medicines. The chart shows you on edoxaban, sixty milligrams, for the atrial fibrillation.
PT: It's not sixty anymore. My heart doctor cut it down — thirty now. He said my kidneys weren't keeping up. That was a couple months back.
DR: Thirty milligrams — okay, that's a very important correction, thank you. And when did you last take it?
PT: This morning, same as always. Nobody told me to stop.
DR: Understood — so no doses held yet. We'll sort out exactly when to pause it before the procedure, and I'll coordinate that with your cardiologist because of your kidneys. Are you taking any other blood thinners?
PT: Just the fish oil. My neighbor swears by it — calls it a natural blood thinner. One a day.
DR: Good to know — the fish oil is different from the edoxaban, but I'll note it. Still taking the water pill, the furosemide?
PT: Every morning, yes. For the heart.
DR: And any allergies to medications?
PT: Codeine. It makes me sick to my stomach and itchy. It's never been a good one for me.
DR: I'll get that added — nothing's listed right now. So here's the plan: an upper scope and a colonoscopy together, fairly soon given the anemia. We'll talk about whether you need any blood beforehand, and I'll arrange the timing of holding the edoxaban with cardiology.
PT: Alright. Whatever we need to do.
note:
**Subjective:** Mr. Fitzgerald is an 81-year-old man referred for pre-procedure evaluation prior to urgent bidirectional endoscopy (EGD + colonoscopy) for iron-deficiency anemia and guaiac-positive stool. He reports progressive fatigue and new dyspnea on exertion. On directed questioning he reports ~2 weeks of black, tarry stools (melena, not previously documented), concerning for an upper GI source. Past history: permanent atrial fibrillation (followed by cardiology), HFpEF, CKD stage 3a.

Medication reconciliation revealed several discrepancies. The chart/pharmacy list shows edoxaban 60 mg daily; the patient reports his cardiologist reduced edoxaban to 30 mg daily approximately 2 months ago for declining renal function (consistent with the 2026-05-14 cardiology note). He took his edoxaban this morning — no dose has been held. He also takes OTC fish oil daily (which he describes as a 'natural blood thinner'), not on the medication list. He confirms furosemide 20 mg each morning. He reports a codeine intolerance (nausea/vomiting, pruritus) not previously documented.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.8 C, HR 90, RR 16, BP 132/76, SpO2 96% on RA; Ht 175 cm (5'9"), Wt 78 kg (172 lb), BMI 25.5. Labs 2026-07-02: hemoglobin 8.4 g/dL (baseline ~12), hematocrit 26.1%, ferritin 8 ug/L, eGFR 48 mL/min/1.73m2; stool guaiac positive. Ambulatory, no active chest pain reported.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Symptomatic iron-deficiency anemia (Hgb 8.4) with guaiac-positive stool and new melena — concern for chronic/subacute GI blood loss, upper source suspected. Plan urgent-tier bidirectional endoscopy (EGD + colonoscopy).
- Anticoagulation: CORRECT edoxaban dose to 30 mg daily per cardiology (chart shows 60 mg — stale). Patient took a dose this morning; hold not yet initiated. Coordinate periprocedural DOAC hold with cardiology, timing keyed to last dose and CKD3a-adjusted clearance (eGFR 48). Note concurrent OTC fish oil.
- Transfusion: discuss threshold with referring/hematology given symptomatic anemia; type and screen; consider pre-procedure transfusion if symptomatic or Hgb declines.
- Iron deficiency: begin/plan IV iron repletion given ongoing blood loss and poor oral absorption context.
- Update medication list: edoxaban 30 mg (not 60 mg); add OTC fish oil.
- Add allergy/intolerance: codeine (nausea/vomiting, pruritus).
- Continue furosemide 20 mg daily; monitor volume status periprocedurally.
- Anesthesia/sedation planning given age, HFpEF, CKD, and anticoagulation.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• Low blood count (anemia) and blood in the stool
• New dark, tarry bowel movements and feeling more tired/short of breath
• Planning an upper scope (EGD) and a colonoscopy together, soon
• Your blood thinner (edoxaban) and how to manage it around the procedure

Next steps
• We will schedule your EGD and colonoscopy soon; the team will send prep instructions.
• Your edoxaban dose was corrected in our records to 30 mg daily (the old 60 mg was out of date). We will tell you exactly when to stop it before the procedure and are coordinating that with your heart doctor because of your kidneys.
• We added your codeine intolerance and noted the fish oil you take.
• We will check whether you need a blood transfusion before the procedure and plan to give iron through the vein.
• Keep taking your furosemide each morning unless we tell you otherwise.
• Call or seek care right away for lots of bleeding, black stools with dizziness, chest pain, or fainting.
